perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by the Laws of England (English common and statutory Law).
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by the Laws of England (English common and statutory Law) excluding the provisions of the Sale of Goods Act 1979.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding the Sale of Goods Act 1979, the contract creates a contradiction as the Act would normally apply to a supply agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sale of Goods Act 1979 implies terms into contracts for the sale of goods, such as satisfactory quality and fitness for purpose. Excluding this Act removes these protections normally afforded to the buyer (Customer), contradicting the standard legal framework for such agreements in England.

Any information contained in this act summary does not constitute legal advice and should be treated as educational content only. Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Act Summary Introduction This summary examines the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (“the Act”) within its context: why it was drafted, what are its important provisions, and how it has changed since it came into force. It is submitted that the Sale of Goods Act 1979 has been part of a change in consumer dealings, with its most significant contributions being to the rights consumers have where they buy products that turn out to be faulty. Indeed, its use has been so central to implied terms in particular, that one author has been criticised for failing to see the contribution of the Act to the law of implied terms. [1] At the same time, the Act’s importance has to an extent been diminished by the introduction of very recent legislation. [2] Rationale for Drafting The Act codified various provisions, such as the formation of contract, which was already prevalent and well-established in common law. [3] However, its contribution was to enhance consumer confidence. It has been argued that without the Act, and its successors, there would be little protection for consumers. [4] This imbalance of consumer rights would lead to significant caution, and even “defensive consumerism.” [5] If consumers believe they have few rights when purchasing goods, then they are slow to trust newer and less-familiar brands, and will continue to buy goods from established brands, even where those goods actually lack in quality. Therefore, if the legislature does not guarantee c
perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly excludes the Sale of Goods Act 1979, which, according to the provided text from LawTeacher.net, enhances consumer confidence and provides rights to consumers regarding faulty products. By excluding this Act, the contract removes protections normally afforded to the buyer, directly contradicting the standard legal framework.

perturbation.original_text: The Manufacturer covenants that the Products are clear of any Intellectual Property claims by third parties and that the Customer has full rights to sell and market the Products worldwide. The Manufacturer indemnifies the Customer for the same.
perturbation.changed_text: The Manufacturer covenants that the Products are mostly clear of any Intellectual Property claims by third parties and that the Customer has the right to sell and market the Products worldwide, so long as sales do not affect pre-existing distribution agreements in other countries. The Manufacturer indemnifies the Customer for the same, but only for direct losses.
perturbation.explanation: The changes introduce uncertainty and conflict regarding intellectual property rights. Using "mostly clear" and limiting indemnity to "direct losses" creates ambiguity. The additional clause about pre-existing distribution agreements affects rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Marks Act 1994 provides clarity on trademark rights and infringement. The original text guarantees the customer full rights to sell and market worldwide, and full indemnification. By adding limitations, the changed text introduces possible conflicts with the Act if the Customer infringes a trademark, or is not fully indemnified, as it contradicts the original guarantee of rights. The phrase 'mostly clear' makes it unclear if the manufacturer did proper due diligence.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it weakens the original guarantee regarding intellectual property rights. The original text states the Products are 'clear of any Intellectual Property claims' while the changed text states they are 'mostly clear'. The use of "mostly clear" creates ambiguity. The scraped text references the Trade Marks Act 1994, supporting the importance of clarity around trademark rights. Additionally, limiting indemnity to only "direct losses" weakens the customer's protection compared to the original agreement's full indemnification. The additional clause allowing sales so long as they don't affect pre-existing distribution agreements also affects rights. This ambiguity and reduced protection creates a potential legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement is for a term of ten (10) years. 1.3 Renewal This agreement will be automatically renewed at the end of every ten (10) year term, with each subsequent term of renewal being for a ten (10) year term. A six (6) months notice must be given by either party of their intention to terminate relations due to any reason other than breach of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement is for a term of ten (10) years. 1.3 Renewal This agreement will be automatically renewed at the end of every ten (10) year term, with each subsequent term of renewal being for a ten (10) year term. Notice to terminate relations is optional.
perturbation.explanation: By making notice to terminate 'optional,' it creates a legal contradiction because optionality of notice would lead to immediate termination without proper procedure, which could cause damages or legal disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law principles of contract termination
perturbation.law_explanation: English common law typically implies a requirement of reasonable notice for termination of contracts, even if not explicitly stated. Making notice 'optional' undermines this principle, potentially leading to a breach of implied terms if one party terminates the contract abruptly without giving the other party sufficient time to make alternative arrangements.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires a six-month notice for termination, but the changed version makes this notice 'optional.' This directly contradicts the principle of reasonable notice, potentially leading to damages or legal disputes. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both emphasize that making notice optional undermines the requirement of reasonable notice, potentially resulting in a breach of implied terms.
